skill.name: timescaledb
skill.description: TimescaleDB guidance for PostgreSQL time-series schemas, hypertables, compression, continuous aggregates, and query performance.
skill.summary: Plan, tune, and troubleshoot TimescaleDB time-series workloads.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, credential, and sensitive-keyword patterns across generated Markdown reference files. Targeted review found these are passive TimescaleDB documentation examples, URLs, placeholders, and ordinary database terms, not executable skill code or prompt injection. The remaining risk is that users may copy privileged installation commands without validating them.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Installation Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The reference material includes sudo package-management commands and shell installer examples. These are normal TimescaleDB installation instructions, but users should verify package sources before copying them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in fenced Markdown examples and include sudo or curl-to-shell patterns. Confidence is high that they are passive documentation, not automatically executed code.

low_findings.title: Credential Placeholder Examples
low_findings.description: Documentation shows placeholders for DATABASE_URL and Basic authorization headers. No real secrets were found, but users should avoid placing production credentials in prompts or shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain explicit placeholder syntax and environment variable references. I found no evidence of embedded real credentials or exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: Static hits for Terraform state, Windows SAM, C2 terms, browser storage, and weak cryptography map to ordinary documentation words such as Terraform, SAML, self-hosted, storage, UUID, and ROW LEVEL SECURITY.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted context shows documentation prose rather than secret files, malware terms, or browser storage access. Confidence is high, but the generated corpus is large.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Installer Example
dangerous_patterns.description: A documentation example pipes a remote Homebrew installer into bash. This is not executed by the skill, but it is a pattern users should inspect before running.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell pattern is present in a fenced Markdown block. The surrounding file is generated reference documentation, so the risk is user-copy risk rather than skill execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Package Installation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include sudo package installation and repository setup commands for TimescaleDB. They are expected for database installation documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are visible and require elevated privileges if copied. There is no evidence that the skill runs them automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Authentication Header Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The API reference demonstrates Basic authorization using placeholder access and secret keys. This is a documentation pattern, not hardcoded credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders and appear inside HTTP and bash examples. I found no real key material or network exfiltration logic.

content.value_statement: Time-series PostgreSQL systems need schema choices that match retention, ingest rate, and query patterns. This skill helps teams apply TimescaleDB documentation to hypertables, compression, continuous aggregates, and performance tuning.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use hypertables, chunks, compression, and continuous aggregates.
Helps design time-series schemas for metrics, events, IoT data, and observability workloads.
Summarizes TimescaleDB installation, connection, and migration documentation.
Guides query optimization using time buckets, hyperfunctions, indexes, and retention policies.
Compares self-hosted and Tiger Cloud documentation paths for common database tasks.
limitations: It does not connect to a live database or inspect actual query plans unless you provide them.
It cannot verify that copied installation commands match your operating system or package source policy.
It may reflect the bundled documentation version rather than the latest upstream TimescaleDB release.
It does not replace database administrator review for production migrations or security controls.

use_cases.title: Design a Metrics Schema
use_cases.description: Choose hypertable dimensions, chunk intervals, and indexes for high-volume metrics data.

use_cases.title: Tune Retention and Compression
use_cases.description: Plan compression policies, retention windows, and query patterns for older time-series data.

use_cases.title: Explain Time-Series Features
use_cases.description: Translate TimescaleDB concepts into clear guidance for product analytics or observability projects.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain TimescaleDB hypertables, chunks, and time buckets for a developer who already knows PostgreSQL.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a TimescaleDB schema for [data type], with expected ingest rate [rate] and common queries [queries].

prompt_templates.title: Tune Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TimescaleDB table design and query pattern. Suggest indexes, chunk intervals, compression settings, and continuous aggregates.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production migration checklist for moving PostgreSQL time-series tables to TimescaleDB, including rollback and validation steps.

output_examples.input: I need to store sensor readings from 20,000 devices every minute.
output: Recommended hypertable structure with time and device dimensions.
Chunk interval guidance based on ingest rate and retention period.
Index and compression suggestions for device-level queries.

output_examples.input: Our daily dashboard query is slow over six months of data.
output: Continuous aggregate strategy for dashboard granularity.
Refresh policy guidance and data-latency tradeoffs.
Checks for indexes, chunk exclusion, and compression impact.

output_examples.input: We need a safe TimescaleDB installation plan for staging.
output: Operating-system and package-source checks before installation.
Database extension enablement steps and validation commands.

best_practices: Provide table schemas, query patterns, ingest rates, and retention goals before asking for tuning advice.
Validate installation commands against official package sources and your internal security policy.
Test compression, retention, and continuous aggregate policies on staging data before production rollout.
anti_patterns: Do not copy privileged shell commands from documentation without reviewing package sources and operating system targets.
Do not choose chunk intervals without considering ingest volume, query windows, and retention needs.
Do not share production connection strings, API keys, or database passwords in prompts.

faq.question: Does this skill run TimescaleDB commands for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation-based guidance and examples. You decide which commands to run in your environment.

faq.question: Can it help with plain PostgreSQL?
faq.answer: Yes, when the task relates to time-series schema design, indexing, or migration to TimescaleDB features.

faq.question: Does it cover Tiger Cloud and self-hosted TimescaleDB?
faq.answer: Yes. The bundled references include cloud, self-hosted, installation, API, and integration material.

faq.question: Can it tune a real slow query?
faq.answer: It can suggest tuning steps if you provide schema, query text, indexes, data volume, and an execution plan.

faq.question: Is the documentation always current?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses bundled reference files, so verify version-specific details against current official documentation.

faq.question: What should I avoid sharing?
faq.answer: Avoid sharing passwords, connection strings, API keys, private hostnames, and customer data unless they are redacted.
